errors.generic: Something went wrong. Please try again.
errors.validation: Please check the entered fields and try again.

common.shareFallback: Copy this link to save your scenario:

search.empty: No company found. You can enter an exact ticker and press Calculate.
search.error: Search is temporarily unavailable. Enter an exact ticker and press Calculate.

invest.title: Invest Calculator
invest.description: Project recurring investments, returns, inflation, and contribution growth over time.

helpers.currency: Choose the currency you use for the goal. The calculator does not convert exchange rates.
helpers.years: How many years you plan to invest regularly.
helpers.startingCapital: Money you already have today. Leave 0 if you are starting from scratch.
helpers.contribution: How much you can realistically add at the selected frequency.
helpers.frequency: Monthly is usually enough for a first scenario.
helpers.return: A rough expected average annual return before inflation.
helpers.inflation: Used to estimate the future purchasing power of the result.
helpers.contributionGrowth: If you plan to raise contributions with income, enter the annual increase. Otherwise leave 0.

advanced.title: Advanced settings
advanced.summary: Currently using {{inflation}}% inflation and {{contributionGrowth}}% annual contribution growth.

explain.totalContributions: Money you personally add over the whole period.
explain.netProfit: Projected growth after subtracting your contributions.
explain.nominalValue: Future amount in the selected currency before adjusting for inflation.
explain.inflationLoss: How much purchasing power inflation removes from the nominal result.

guide.title: How to read this result
guide.text: Compare real value with nominal value. Real value is usually the better planning number because it estimates future purchasing power.

fire.title: FIRE Calculator
fire.description: Estimate the portfolio needed for financial independence and the timeline to reach it.

helpers.currency: Choose the currency you use for FIRE planning. The calculator does not convert exchange rates.

explain.currentPortfolio: What you already have invested today.
explain.totalContributions: New money added before reaching the FIRE target.
explain.growthProfit: Projected portfolio growth from investment returns.
explain.passiveIncome: Estimated monthly spending supported by the withdrawal rate.

rule.title: 4% Rule — Trinity Study
rule.description: The classic rule suggests a diversified portfolio can often support annual withdrawals near 4% of the starting balance. Use it as a planning shortcut, not a guarantee.

dca.title: DCA Simulator
dca.description: Simulate recurring monthly investments using historical market prices when available.

helpers.ticker: Example: SPY is an ETF that tracks the S&P 500 index.

hero.helper: Uses historical market prices when available.

explain.invested: Total money contributed over the simulated period.
explain.return: Percentage gain or loss on the money invested.
explain.averagePrice: Blended purchase price across all monthly buys.

guide.title: What DCA shows
guide.text: DCA tests consistency, not perfect timing. A strong result means regular buying worked in this historical path; it does not guarantee the next path.

notice.title: Estimated data active
notice.fallback: Live history for {{ticker}} is unavailable right now, so this run uses estimated prices.
notice.backendFallback: Live market history is unavailable right now, so this run uses estimated prices.

noData.title: History not found
noData.text: Lucrum could not find real historical prices for {{ticker}}. Check the ticker or try another asset.

fairPrice.title: Fair Price
fairPrice.description: Estimate a company through EPS DCF, P/E, or P/S scenarios with a margin of safety.

helpers.eps: Profit per share over the last 12 months. Use it mainly for companies that already earn steady profits.
helpers.marketPrice: The current share price used for comparison with your model result.
helpers.growth: Your estimate for annual profit growth over the next five years.
helpers.terminalGrowth: A conservative long-term growth rate after year five.
helpers.discount: A higher rate makes the model stricter about future money and risk.
helpers.targetPe: The earnings multiple you think the company could reasonably deserve.
helpers.targetPs: The sales multiple you think the company could reasonably deserve.
helpers.revenuePerShare: Annual revenue divided by shares. Useful when profits are still developing.

lookup.title: Ticker data
lookup.updated: Data for {{ticker}} was loaded. Review the model and assumptions before relying on the result.
lookup.partial: Found {{ticker}}, but these fields are missing: {{fields}}. Unavailable values were cleared so old data from another company does not leak into the model.
lookup.noData: Live data is unavailable for {{ticker}} right now. You can still edit EPS and market price manually.
lookup.notFound: Could not find reliable data for {{ticker}}. Check the ticker or try another symbol. Details: {{message}}

growthHint.title: Growth company detected
growthHint.body: Companies like Amazon or Tesla reinvest most profits, making EPS very low. Analysts typically use Free Cash Flow per share (FCF/share) instead of EPS for more accurate valuation. You can find FCF/share on financial sites like Macrotrends or Simply Wall St.

fairPrice.epsTooltip: Use EPS for stable companies. For high-growth companies, compare this result with revenue, margins and cash flow before trusting it.

financial.title: Bank or fintech profile detected
financial.text: A pure EPS DCF can be too blunt for banks, lenders and fintechs. Revenue growth, loan quality, deposits, ROE and operating leverage can matter more than today’s EPS, so Lucrum switches to a multiple scenario when enough data is available.

growth.title: Growth profile detected
growth.text: Current EPS can understate companies that reinvest heavily. Treat the DCF as a conservative stress test and compare it with a P/E or P/S scenario.

lowEps.title: Low EPS signal
lowEps.text: When EPS is small relative to price, an EPS DCF can look harsh even if the market is pricing future margin expansion. Use a multiple scenario to separate business growth from today’s profit base.

modeInfo.title: {{mode}} active
modeInfo.dcf: Best for profitable, stable businesses where EPS already reflects the business engine. It is intentionally conservative for early growth stories.
modeInfo.pe: Useful when you believe the company will deserve a specific earnings multiple. Fair price = EPS × target P/E.
modeInfo.ps: Useful for growth, fintech and low-margin companies where revenue scale matters before mature earnings. Fair price = revenue per share × target P/S.

explain.marketPrice: The price the market is currently showing.
explain.upside: Distance between market price and the model's safe-buy price.
explain.forecastPv: Present value of the first five forecast years.
explain.terminalPv: Present value of the value after year five.
explain.currentPe: How many dollars investors pay for one dollar of earnings today.
explain.targetPe: The earnings multiple you assume could be fair.
explain.currentPs: How many dollars investors pay for one dollar of sales today.
explain.targetPs: The sales multiple you assume could be fair.

verdict.undervaluedText: The market price is below the safety-adjusted buy price.
verdict.overvaluedText: The market price is above the safety-adjusted buy price.

formula.title: Formula
formula.text: Sum PV (years 1-5) + Terminal PV = Fair Price.
formula.dcf: Sum PV (years 1-5) + Terminal PV = Fair Price.
formula.pe: EPS (TTM) × target P/E = Fair Price. Safety-adjusted buy price then subtracts the margin of safety.
formula.ps: Revenue per share × target P/S = Fair Price. Safety-adjusted buy price then subtracts the margin of safety.

how.title: How it works
how.text: Think of a company like an apple tree. EPS is the apples it produces today, growth estimates more apples in the next five years, and the discount rate asks what those future apples are worth in today's money.
how.dcf: Think of a company like an apple tree. EPS is the apples it produces today, growth estimates more apples in the next five years, and the discount rate asks what those future apples are worth in today's money.
how.pe: Choose the earnings multiple you think the market could reasonably pay. This is simpler than DCF and makes the assumption visible: the result depends on both EPS quality and the target P/E.
how.ps: Choose the sales multiple you think the business could deserve. This can fit companies where revenue scale is clear but profits are still ramping, but it must be checked against margins and cash flow.

beginner.title: What do these numbers mean?
beginner.description: This calculator does not tell you to buy or sell. It shows the price implied by your selected model and how far the market is from that price.

fairPrice.title: Fair price
fairPrice.text: An estimated share price from the selected model. It is not a forecast or guarantee, just the output of your assumptions.

safeBuy.title: Safe buy price
safeBuy.text: Fair price minus the margin of safety. It is lower so there is room for errors in the data or forecast.

margin.title: Margin of safety
margin.text: A discount to the model price. 30% means you only consider buying if the market is roughly one-third below your estimate.

eps.title: EPS
eps.text: Earnings per share. If a company earns money steadily, EPS can be a useful base for valuation.

pe.title: P/E
pe.text: How much the market pays for 1 dollar of earnings. A higher P/E means the market expects more from the future.

ps.title: P/S
ps.text: How much the market pays for 1 dollar of revenue. It can help with companies whose profits are still uneven.

dcf.title: DCF
dcf.text: An attempt to estimate what future profits are worth today. Bolder assumptions create bolder results.

discount.title: Discount rate
discount.text: The higher it is, the stricter the model is about future money. It is a way to account for time and risk.

steps.title: How to use it without a finance degree
steps.chooseModel: Start with a model you understand: DCF or P/E for stable profitable companies, often P/S for fintech and growth companies.
steps.checkInputs: Check the input numbers. If EPS, revenue, or price looks strange, the result will be strange too.
steps.comparePrices: Compare the market price with the safe buy price, not only with the fair price.
steps.useCaution: If upside is negative, it is not an order to sell. It means the price is high under your current assumptions.

warning.title: When not to trust the result
warning.text: Do not rely on one model for banks, unprofitable growth companies, cyclical businesses, or tickers with incomplete live data. Compare several scenarios and understand the business, not only the number.

stock.title: Stock Analysis
stock.description: Review company fundamentals and a simple valuation snapshot.

explain.pe: Price compared with current earnings. Lower is not always better.
explain.forwardPE: Price compared with expected future earnings. Empty means data is unavailable.
explain.eps: Earnings per share: profit assigned to one share.
explain.fcf: Cash left after operating needs and capital spending.
explain.priceToSales: Price compared with revenue. Useful when profits are still maturing.
explain.priceToBook: Price compared with accounting book value.
explain.pegRatio: P/E adjusted for growth. Empty means growth data is unavailable.
explain.beta: Volatility compared with the market. 1 is roughly market-like.
explain.grossMargin: Revenue left after direct production costs.
explain.currentRatio: Short-term assets divided by short-term liabilities.

insights.none: No clear signals from available data.

insightItems.highPe: Very high P/E — significant growth priced in

funds.notice: This ticker appears to be a fund or ETF. Company metrics such as EPS, P/E, revenue and ROE usually do not apply, so Lucrum shows market quote data instead.
funds.quoteOnlyNotice: Lucrum found live quote data, but company fundamentals are unavailable for this ticker. The page shows the market fields that could be verified.
funds.marketSnapshotText: Funds and ETFs are analyzed through holdings, fees, tracking and portfolio fit. This view keeps live quote fields visible without forcing company-style fundamentals.

undervalued.title: Potentially undervalued
undervalued.text: The P/E ratio is below 15, which can signal a cheaper valuation if the business quality is intact.

fair.title: Fairly valued
fair.text: The P/E ratio sits between 15 and 25, a common range for mature companies with steady earnings.

premium.title: Premium valuation
premium.text: The P/E ratio is above 25, so future growth needs to justify the higher price.

highGrowth.title: High growth premium
highGrowth.text: The P/E ratio is above 50, which usually means investors are paying for very strong future growth.

unknown.title: Not enough data
unknown.text: Lucrum needs a valid P/E ratio before showing a valuation assessment.

notice.title: Estimated data active
notice.fallback: Some live data is unavailable, so Lucrum filled the gaps with estimates.
notice.readyText: Enter a ticker or company name and Lucrum will load the available market data.
notice.partial: Some company fields are unavailable right now, so only verified data is shown.
notice.noFinancials: Revenue and net income are unavailable for this ticker.
notice.noEpsHistory: EPS history is unavailable for this ticker.

hero.subtext: Plan your next move with simple calculators, fair value checks, and stock insights built for long-term investors.

tools.title: Choose the right calculator.
tools.description: Model goals, compare strategies, and understand stocks without needing a finance degree.

investCalc.title: Investment calculator
investCalc.description: Estimate future portfolio value from deposits, returns, and time horizon.

fire.title: FIRE: financial independence
fire.description: Find the savings rate and timeline that can support financial independence.

dca.title: Regular investing (DCA)
dca.description: Simulate recurring investments and compare market timing outcomes.

fairPrice.title: Fair Price
fairPrice.description: Run a beginner-friendly fair value check using fundamentals and assumptions.

stock.title: Stock Analysis
stock.description: Look up a ticker and review key company metrics in one focused view.

afterTools.title: A calculator should start a question, not end it.
afterTools.description: Lucrum pairs numbers with education, assumptions and plain-language cautions so beginners do not treat a single output as a command.

education.title: Academy beside the tools
education.text: Each result points back to concepts you can learn before acting.

transparent.title: Assumptions stay visible
transparent.text: Growth, inflation, discount rates and safety margins are inputs you can challenge.

privacy.title: No hidden advice
privacy.text: Lucrum is educational software, not a broker or financial advisor.

toolsDirectory.title: All investment tools in one place.
toolsDirectory.description: Start with a goal, test assumptions, then inspect a company or strategy before making your next move.

workflow.title: Suggested flow
workflow.plan: Plan long-term growth with Invest Calc or FIRE.
workflow.decide: Check valuation with Fair Price and Stock Analysis.

investCalc.detail: Best for seeing how starting capital, contributions, inflation, and growth compound over decades.

fire.detail: Best for estimating your independence target, progress, and years left at a chosen withdrawal rate.

dca.detail: Best for testing monthly investing into a ticker using historical prices when available.

fairPrice.detail: Best for turning EPS, growth, discount rate, and margin of safety into a simple buy-price range.

stock.detail: Best for a quick company snapshot with fundamentals, profitability, leverage, and a valuation verdict.

academy.title: Not sure what the output means?
academy.description: Open the Academy for short explanations of risk, asset allocation, valuation, DCA and the terms that show up inside the calculators.

beginner.title: Built for beginners
beginner.text: The tools favor simple scenarios and readable assumptions over opaque recommendations.

assumptions.title: You control the model
assumptions.text: Change every important input and see how the result moves before trusting it.

data.title: Data is labeled
data.text: When live market data is unavailable, Lucrum tells you instead of pretending.

glossary.title: Terms you will see
glossary.description: These short definitions reduce the first-click confusion before you open a detailed lesson.

cagr.definition: Average annual growth rate after compounding.

pv.definition: Present value: future money translated into today's value.

nominal.definition: Nominal ignores inflation. Real adjusts for purchasing power.

swr.definition: Safe withdrawal rate used in FIRE planning.

pricing.title: Start free. Upgrade when you need more signal.
pricing.description: Lucrum is free for core planning. Pro adds saved workspaces, watchlists, exports, and richer market data when you are ready.

stub.title: Pricing is not available yet.
stub.description: For now, Lucrum is focused on the calculators, learning pages, and the first version of the investor workspace.
stub.cardTitle: No paid plans are active right now
stub.cardText: We will add pricing only after the product is useful enough to deserve it. Until then, keep using the available tools freely.

items.account: Accounts and saved workspaces are still being shaped.
items.notice: Paid plans will be announced clearly before anything changes.

free.name: Free
free.description: For learning the basics and running quick calculations without an account.

features.mock: Estimated fallback data when live data is unavailable

pro.name: Pro
pro.description: For investors who want to save scenarios, track tickers, and make cleaner decisions.

founder.name: Founder
founder.description: For early supporters who want lifetime access to the Pro workspace.

compare.title: Pick the workspace that fits your pace.
compare.description: The free tier stays useful. Paid plans are for saving, tracking, and going deeper.

learn.title: A calm place to understand the basics.
learn.description: Short answers for beginner investors: how compounding works, why DCA matters, what FIRE means, and how to think about fair value.

rule.title: Simple rule
rule.text: Lucrum is for planning and education. It helps you compare assumptions, not predict the future.

compound.title: Compounding
compound.text: Small contributions can become meaningful when time, return, and consistency work together.

dca.title: Dollar-cost averaging
dca.text: DCA means investing a fixed amount regularly, so you buy more shares when prices are lower and fewer when prices are higher.

valuation.title: Fair value
valuation.text: A stock can be a great company and still be too expensive. Valuation helps you estimate the price that gives you a margin of safety.

study.title: What to learn first
study.description: A practical order for building confidence before you compare stocks or chase complex strategies.

budget.title: Know how much you can invest
budget.text: Start with a monthly amount you can repeat without breaking your everyday budget.

fire.title: Set a target
fire.text: Estimate the portfolio size that could support your expenses and turn a vague dream into a number.

dca.title: Practice regular investing
dca.text: Use DCA to see how consistency behaves across different market paths and time periods.

review.title: Review the business
review.text: Check revenue, profit, cash flow, debt, valuation, and whether the story still makes sense.

cagr.definition: Compound annual growth rate. It shows the smoothed yearly growth rate over a period.

eps.definition: Earnings per share. It tells you how much profit belongs to one share.

pe.definition: Price divided by earnings. It is a quick way to compare price with current profits.

fcf.definition: Cash left after a company pays for operations and necessary investments.

swr.definition: Safe withdrawal rate. FIRE plans often use it to estimate sustainable yearly withdrawals.

margin.definition: A buffer between your estimated value and the price you are willing to pay.

resources.title: External sources worth bookmarking
resources.description: Neutral resources for basics, calculators, market data, and economic context.

investorIntro.title: Investor.gov: introduction to investing
investorIntro.text: A beginner-friendly SEC resource on goals, risk, diversification, and investor protection.

compoundCalc.title: Investor.gov compound interest calculator
compoundCalc.text: A simple official calculator for testing time, contributions, and expected return.

finraBasics.title: FINRA investing basics
finraBasics.text: Plain-language guidance on asset classes, risk, diversification, and market basics.

fred.title: FRED economic data
fred.text: A free database from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis for inflation, rates, GDP, and more.

faq.title: Beginner questions
faq.description: Quick answers before you open a calculator.

start.question: Where should I start?
start.answer: Start with your goal. If you want long-term growth, use Invest Calc. If you want financial independence, use FIRE. If you want to test monthly investing, use DCA.

dca.question: Is DCA better than investing all at once?
dca.answer: Not always. Lump-sum investing can win when markets rise quickly, but DCA is easier emotionally and reduces the risk of investing everything right before a drop.

fire.question: What is FIRE?
fire.answer: FIRE means Financial Independence, Retire Early. The basic idea is to build a portfolio large enough to cover expenses through withdrawals.

fairPrice.question: What is a fair price?
fairPrice.answer: A fair price is an estimate of what a business might be worth today based on future earnings, growth, and risk. It is an estimate, not a precise truth.

risk.question: What is margin of safety?
risk.answer: Margin of safety means buying below your estimated fair value so small mistakes in your assumptions do not immediately break the investment case.

data.question: Are calculator results financial advice?
data.answer: No. They are educational planning tools. Use them to understand scenarios, then do deeper research before investing.

learnResources.title: Useful links
learnResources.description: Moderator-curated links that add practical context to this lesson.
learnResources.error: Useful links are unavailable right now.

learnAcademy.title: Learn the main asset classes before you choose a tool.
learnAcademy.description: A practical beginner map for bonds, stocks, funds, crypto, venture investing, and the risk basics that connect them.

note.title: Educational, not advice
note.text: These guides explain concepts, trade-offs, and risks. They do not recommend buying any specific asset.

goal.title: Start with the goal
goal.text: A short-term cash need, a 20-year retirement plan, and a high-risk experiment should not use the same asset mix.

risk.title: Name the risk
risk.text: Every asset has a different way to disappoint you: price drops, inflation, default, fees, scams, or no liquidity.

time.title: Match the time horizon
time.text: The more time you have, the easier it is to tolerate volatility. The less time you have, the more stability matters.

catalog.title: Pick a topic
catalog.description: Each section explains what the asset is, how it can make money or preserve value, what can go wrong, and which terms matter.

miniCourse.title: 17 steps before your first stock
miniCourse.description: A full original 17-part beginner path through financial foundations, risk profile, asset choice, ETFs, business analysis, statements, valuation, entry methods, and technical indicators.
miniCourse.previewOneText: The course starts with personal finances because even a good stock cannot fix a weak plan.
miniCourse.previewTwoText: Each lesson includes a practical situation: how to think, what to check, and where beginners often slip.
miniCourse.previewThreeText: The stocks topic stays compact, while the deeper material opens as its own learning path.
miniCourse.ruleText: Do not buy a stock only because it is popular or has dropped. First understand the business, price, risk, time horizon, and the role this position has in your portfolio.
miniCourse.finalText: After the course you should have a short investment note: why this company, how it makes money, what can go wrong, what valuation you accept, what your horizon is, and what would make you change your mind. If you cannot write that note in simple words, it is probably too early to buy.

notFound.title: This lesson is not available.
notFound.description: The academy page has the current list of investing sections.

bonds.title: Bonds
bonds.summary: Loans to governments or companies that usually pay interest and return principal at maturity.
bonds.why: Useful for income, planning known dates, and reducing portfolio volatility, but not risk-free.
bonds.intro: A bond is a loan. You lend money to a government, city, or company. In return, the issuer usually pays interest and promises to repay the principal on a maturity date.

sections.what: Bonds are debt securities. The buyer becomes a lender, not an owner. The bond contract defines the coupon, maturity, and repayment priority.
sections.earn: Returns usually come from coupon payments, repayment at maturity, and price changes if rates move after you buy.
sections.risks: Bond prices can fall when interest rates rise. Issuers can also default, and inflation can reduce the real value of fixed payments.
sections.mistakes: A high yield is not automatically a bargain. It can mean the market sees higher credit or duration risk.
sections.fit: Bonds may fit money you want to stabilize, income needs, or goals with a clearer date than long-term stock growth.

risks.rates: Interest-rate risk: existing bond prices often fall when new bonds offer higher yields.
risks.credit: Credit risk: the issuer may struggle to pay interest or repay principal.
risks.inflation: Inflation risk: fixed payments can buy less in the future.

mistakes.yieldOnly: Choosing only the highest yield without asking why the yield is high.
mistakes.duration: Ignoring duration and being surprised when a bond fund falls after rates rise.
mistakes.cash: Treating bonds like cash even though market prices can move.

fit.income: You want more predictable interest income.
fit.stability: You want part of the portfolio to move differently from stocks.
fit.knownDate: You are matching money to a future date, like tuition or a home deposit.

coupon.definition: The stated interest payment a bond pays, usually shown as an annual percentage of face value.

yield.definition: The return implied by the bond price, coupon, and time to maturity.

maturity.definition: The date when the issuer is expected to repay the bond principal.

duration.definition: A measure of how sensitive a bond or bond fund is to interest-rate changes.

stocks.title: Stocks
stocks.summary: Ownership stakes in companies, with returns driven by business performance, expectations, and dividends.
stocks.why: Stocks can build long-term wealth, but the price can move far more than the company changes in a single year.
stocks.intro: A share of stock represents partial ownership in a company. If the business grows, earns more, and the market values it well, shareholders can benefit.

sections.what: Stocks are equity. You own a small claim on a business, not a fixed promise of repayment.
sections.earn: Returns can come from price appreciation, dividends, buybacks, and growth in earnings or cash flow.
sections.risks: A company can disappoint, get disrupted, take on too much debt, or simply become too expensive relative to its profits.
sections.mistakes: A familiar brand is not automatically a good investment. The price you pay still matters.
sections.fit: Stocks may fit long-term growth goals where you can tolerate volatility and research business quality.

foundation.title: Prepare your financial base
foundation.text: Before stocks, you need an emergency fund, debt control, and a clear view of monthly cash flow. Otherwise the market can force you to sell at the worst time.
foundation.details: Stocks move around. Even a strong company can fall 20-40% because of the market, rates, an earnings report, or investor fear. If you do not have a reserve, an ordinary life problem can force you to sell for cash instead of because the investment thesis changed. The financial base is not boring paperwork; it protects you from forced decisions.
foundation.example: Imagine someone invests $1,000 in a stock and two months later loses part of their income or needs to replace a laptop. Without a reserve, they may sell at a loss even if the business is still healthy. Someone with a reserve can separate a life problem from an investing decision.
foundation.caseStudy: Mia earns $1,800 a month and spends about $1,200. She wants to invest all $3,000 of savings at once because she fears missing the next rally. A steadier path is to keep 3-6 months of expenses as a reserve, remove expensive debt, and invest from future monthly cash flow. Then stocks do not compete with basic safety.
foundation.action: Build a reserve for 3-6 months of expenses, list high-interest debts, and calculate the amount you can realistically invest each month.
foundation.checkOne: The emergency reserve is not stored in stocks.
foundation.checkTwo: High-interest debt is not eating more than investments can reasonably earn.
foundation.checkThree: The monthly investing amount does not break the budget or create stress.
foundation.warning: Do not invest money that may be needed soon for rent, healthcare, education, taxes, or other required payments.

plan.title: Write a personal plan
plan.text: An investing plan answers three questions: why the money is invested, when it is needed, and how much drawdown you can realistically tolerate.
plan.details: Without a plan, an investor keeps reacting to noise: news, chats, red days, and other people's gains. A plan should define the goal, horizon, contribution rhythm, maximum size of one stock, and selling rules. It does not guarantee profit, but it reduces chaos. A good plan is short enough to read in a minute when the market feels emotional.
plan.example: A plan can say: I invest for 10 years, add $300 each month, keep one stock below 8% of the portfolio, buy only profitable and understandable businesses, and sell because the thesis breaks rather than because the price fell.
plan.caseStudy: Owen bought three popular stocks after a video. One dropped 18%, and he did not know whether to buy more, sell, or wait. With a plan, the decision would be clearer: position below 5%, add only after checking the report, sell only if revenue weakens or the company loses its edge.
plan.action: Write your goal, horizon, recurring contribution, maximum position size, and selling rule.
plan.checkOne: The goal has a horizon, not just the phrase make money.
plan.checkTwo: Each position has a reason beyond a good-looking chart.
plan.checkThree: The selling rule is written before the purchase.
plan.warning: A plan that changes after every video or post is not a plan. It is an emotional reaction with a better name.

truthsMyths.title: Truths and myths about investing
truthsMyths.text: Investing is not a quick-rich button. It is a system of decisions where patience, risk control, and entry price matter more than loud promises.
truthsMyths.details: The sweet myth says you only need to find one stock that will make you rich. The harder truth is less glamorous: most results come from discipline, diversification, risk control, a long horizon, and the ability to keep a plan when the market makes you look wrong. The market does not pay for emotions, ticker popularity, or confidence in a chat. It rewards buying quality assets at reasonable prices and surviving uncomfortable periods.
truthsMyths.example: A stock can rise 80% in one year and still be a poor buy today if the price already assumes a perfect scenario. A broad ETF may look less exciting, but it can be a better fit for someone building capital over 10-20 years.
truthsMyths.caseStudy: Den sees a story about someone buying the bottom and making 5x. He does not see the many similar stories that ended badly. Survivorship bias makes investing look easier than it is. A healthy process asks not only how much can I make, but what happens if the outcome is average or bad.
truthsMyths.action: Write down three investment promises that sound too good, then write the hidden risk next to each one.
truthsMyths.checkOne: You can separate investing from speculation and gambling.
truthsMyths.checkTwo: Expected return is connected to risk, not invented from hope.
truthsMyths.checkThree: Your plan is not built on someone else's success story without looking at losses.
truthsMyths.warning: If an idea is sold as easy money without risk, the risk is usually just unnamed.

riskProfile.title: Define your risk profile
riskProfile.text: Risk is not only price decline. It also includes income shocks, panic, concentration, currency, sector exposure, and mistakes in your assumptions.
riskProfile.details: A risk profile is not about how brave you sound. It is about how you behave when the portfolio really falls. For stocks, think about volatility, concentration, currency exposure, sector dependence, and emotional comfort. Beginners often overestimate their tolerance during good markets and discover their real risk level only in a drawdown.
riskProfile.example: Two people may have the same amount of money but different risk profiles. One has stable income, a reserve, and a 15-year horizon. The other needs a home deposit next year. The same market drop may be tolerable for the first person and damaging for the second.
riskProfile.caseStudy: Ira's portfolio is 80% technology stocks. On paper there are many tickers, but the risk is similar: rates, growth valuations, and sentiment toward tech. If the sector falls, almost everything falls together. Her real risk is higher than the number of companies suggests.
riskProfile.action: Set maximum weights for one stock, one sector, and all risky assets in your portfolio.
riskProfile.checkOne: One stock cannot damage the whole plan by itself.
riskProfile.checkTwo: Different tickers are not all exposed to the same sector risk.
riskProfile.checkThree: You know what you would do if the portfolio fell 20%, 30%, or 40%.
riskProfile.warning: If a 10% fall keeps you awake, do not build a portfolio as if a 50% fall would be easy.

assetMap.title: Compare asset classes
assetMap.text: Stocks give business ownership, bonds behave more like loans, ETFs give a basket, and cash gives flexibility. Each tool has a different job.
assetMap.details: One stock should not replace the whole investing toolkit. Stocks can provide growth, but they charge for it with volatility. Bonds can stabilize part of the portfolio. ETFs offer broad diversification. Cash or short-term instruments provide flexibility. When you understand each asset's job, you are less likely to expect the wrong thing from it.
assetMap.example: If your goal is long-term growth, stocks or ETFs can be the core. If your goal is 18 months away, such as a home deposit, a volatile single stock may be the wrong tool even if you like the company.
assetMap.caseStudy: Andrew has $10,000 and wants to learn stock picking without turning the whole portfolio into a bet. One structure could be 70% broad ETF, 20% bonds or cash, and 10% individual stocks. He gets practice, but the result does not depend on a few decisions.
assetMap.action: Decide whether stocks are your portfolio core, a small active sleeve, or a learning position.
assetMap.checkOne: Short-term goals are not funded with assets that can fall sharply before the spending date.
assetMap.checkTwo: The portfolio has something for stability, not only for growth.
assetMap.checkThree: Individual stocks have a defined role instead of being bought out of boredom.
assetMap.warning: Do not call a risky bet an investment only because it was made through a brokerage app.

compound.title: Understand compounding
compound.text: Long-term returns come from time, reinvestment, and consistency. Large outcomes often look boring at the beginning.
compound.details: Compounding is not magic; it is repetition. Returns start earning future returns. The hard part is that the first years can look slow, so beginners often abandon a systematic strategy and chase a quick jump. Time, regular contributions, dividend reinvestment, and fee control often matter more than one brilliant purchase.
compound.example: When you invest the same amount every month, early results depend more on contributions than returns. Later, the accumulated base starts moving the portfolio more strongly. That is why horizon and discipline matter so much.
compound.caseStudy: Two investors start differently. The first invests $200 every month for 15 years. The second waits five years for the perfect moment and then invests more. Often the first has the edge not because of genius, but because of time in the market. Compounding likes early starts and repetition.
compound.action: Compare 5, 10, and 20 years in the investment calculator with different contributions and expected returns.
compound.checkOne: Dividends and gains have a reinvestment rule.
compound.checkTwo: Expected return does not replace a realistic contribution plan.
compound.checkThree: Fees, taxes, and currency are at least roughly considered.
compound.warning: Do not use unrealistic 30-50% annual returns as your base case. They can make a weak idea look beautiful on a screen.

analysisTypes.title: Separate fundamental and technical analysis
analysisTypes.text: Fundamental analysis asks what the business is and what it may be worth. Technical analysis focuses more on price, trend, and market behavior.
analysisTypes.details: Fundamental analysis starts with the business: what it sells, to whom, at what margin, how it grows, how much debt it carries, and what price the market asks for it. Technical analysis may help with timing or sentiment, but it does not tell you whether the business is good. For a long-term investor, a chart without business understanding becomes guesswork.
analysisTypes.example: A stock may break out to a new high while margins are falling and debt is expensive. Another stock may look weak after a broad market selloff while the company still produces steady cash flow and keeps its competitive advantage.
analysisTypes.caseStudy: Peter buys because a stock breaks out of a range. A week later the company reports slower revenue, lower margins, and weaker guidance. The chart showed price behavior, but it did not replace business analysis. For an investor, those are different jobs.
analysisTypes.action: Explain the business and profit sources first, then look at the chart and entry point.
analysisTypes.checkOne: You can describe the business without opening the price chart.
analysisTypes.checkTwo: A technical signal is not the only reason to buy.
analysisTypes.checkThree: The fundamental thesis has metrics you can monitor.
analysisTypes.warning: Do not use a technical signal as an excuse to buy a company you do not understand.

etfBridge.title: Compare the stock with an ETF
etfBridge.text: If you cannot explain why one company is better than a broad ETF, an index fund may be the simpler and more honest choice.
etfBridge.details: Every single stock has to answer a simple question: why take company-specific risk when you can buy a basket? An ETF does not make you lazy. It gives you a baseline alternative that your idea must beat. If the expected advantage is unclear and the risk is higher, a broad index may be the better decision.
etfBridge.example: If you want one technology company, compare it with a technology ETF or the S&P 500. Is revenue growing faster? Are margins better? Is valuation reasonable? Does everything depend on one product? If the answer is vague, an ETF may be calmer.
etfBridge.caseStudy: Sofia is choosing between one semiconductor company and a sector ETF. The company may rise more, but it depends on a few large customers and the capital-spending cycle. The ETF may be less explosive, but spreads the risk across many businesses. The choice is about risk-adjusted advantage, not excitement.
etfBridge.action: Compare the company with its sector ETF or the S&P 500 on growth, margins, valuation, and risks.
etfBridge.checkOne: There is a clear reason why this company may beat the basket.
etfBridge.checkTwo: You compared concentration risk, not only past returns.
etfBridge.checkThree: If the edge is unclear, the ETF is treated as a valid alternative.
etfBridge.warning: Do not buy one stock only because it feels more exciting than an ETF. More exciting does not always mean better.

qualityCompany.title: Look for a quality business
qualityCompany.text: A strong company has an understandable product, competitive advantage, healthy margins, capital discipline, and management that does not destroy trust.
qualityCompany.details: Business quality is not found in a glossy presentation. It shows up in repeatable signs: the company earns money, keeps customers, has an edge over competitors, does not burn capital without a plan, and can survive weaker cycles. For beginners, an understandable business with stable economics is usually better than a complex story where success depends on one future breakthrough.
qualityCompany.example: A quality business may have a product people buy repeatedly, a strong brand, network effects, lower costs, or high switching costs. But each advantage needs evidence in the numbers: margins, revenue retention, cash flow, and competitor behavior.
qualityCompany.caseStudy: A popular brand is not automatically a quality investment. If sales grow only because marketing expenses explode and margins fall, the brand may be weaker than it looks. Quality appears when the business can earn without endlessly burning money.
qualityCompany.action: Describe the company's advantage in one sentence and find at least two numbers that support it.
qualityCompany.checkOne: The advantage is clear and not just the company is popular.
qualityCompany.checkTwo: Margins, cash flow, or customer retention support the quality claim.
qualityCompany.checkThree: Management is not diluting shareholders or buying growth at any price.
qualityCompany.warning: If the advantage sounds like an ad slogan and does not appear in the financials, it may be a story rather than an edge.

profile.title: Read the company profile
profile.text: Start with sector, geography, revenue sources, customers, and dependencies. If the business is unclear, the numbers are easy to misread.
profile.details: The company profile is the first filter. You should know what the company sells, where it operates, who the customers are, what demand depends on, and what regulatory or currency risks exist. Without that, even good numbers can mislead you: revenue may grow because of a one-off factor, margins may rise because of temporary cost cuts, and profit may improve because of accounting noise.
profile.example: A company may look like a device maker while a major part of profit comes from services. Another may be called fintech but depend heavily on interest rates and credit losses. The profile tells you which metrics actually matter.
profile.caseStudy: Netflix and an industrial equipment maker can both show the same revenue growth, but the business drivers are different. One depends on subscribers, churn, content costs, and attention. The other depends on order cycles, customer capex, and backlog. The same growth number does not mean the same analysis.
profile.action: Write how the company makes money, who the customer is, and the three factors that most affect results.
profile.checkOne: You know which products or segments generate most revenue.
profile.checkTwo: Main dependencies are identified: rates, commodities, regulation, cycle, customers, or currency.
profile.checkThree: You know which metric matters more than headline revenue for this business.
profile.warning: Do not buy a business you cannot explain in plain language to someone outside the market.

statements.title: Review the statements
statements.text: Revenue shows scale, profit shows efficiency, cash flow shows real money power, and the balance sheet shows resilience and debt load.
statements.details: Financial statements should be read as the story of the business. The income statement shows revenue, costs, and profitability. Cash flow shows whether profit turns into real money. The balance sheet shows debt, liquidity, and resilience. One number is rarely enough; the trend over several years and the connection between metrics matter.
statements.example: If revenue grows but net income falls, look for the reason: margins, costs, competition, or one-off charges. If profit exists but free cash flow is weak, the business may need heavy capital spending or have working-capital problems.
statements.caseStudy: A company reports record profit, but free cash flow is close to zero. Money may be stuck in inventory or receivables, or the business may need heavy equipment spending. Profit exists on paper, but owners should care about cash left after maintaining and growing the business.
statements.action: Check 5 years of revenue, net income, free cash flow, debt, and margins.
statements.checkOne: Revenue, profit, and cash flow are reviewed together.
statements.checkTwo: Debt is compared with profit, cash flow, and rates.
statements.checkThree: One-off events are separated from normal operations.
statements.warning: Do not build a thesis from one quarter. A quarter can be noise rather than a new trend.

balanceSheet.title: Break down the balance sheet
balanceSheet.text: The balance sheet shows what a company owns, what it owes, and how much resilience it has before a difficult period.
balanceSheet.details: The balance sheet is often ignored because it is less exciting than revenue or earnings. But it shows debt, cash, inventory, receivables, liabilities, and equity. A company can report attractive profit while having a weak balance sheet: too much short-term debt, too little cash, stuck inventory, or customers that pay slowly. For investors, the balance sheet asks whether the business can survive a bad year without panic moves.
balanceSheet.example: Two companies have the same profit. One has plenty of cash and little debt. The other has large debt that must be refinanced at higher rates. In normal times they may look similar, but in stress the second company has much less freedom.
balanceSheet.caseStudy: A retailer grows quickly and opens stores, but inventory and short-term liabilities rise faster than sales. Growth may be lower quality than it looks: cash is stuck in goods, while suppliers need to be paid before customers bring enough cash back.
balanceSheet.action: Review cash, total debt, short-term liabilities, inventory, and receivables over several years.
balanceSheet.checkOne: Debt is understood by size, maturity, and cost.
balanceSheet.checkTwo: Cash and liquidity are enough for a weaker period.
balanceSheet.checkThree: Inventory and receivables are not growing much faster than revenue without a reason.
balanceSheet.warning: Strong earnings with a weak balance sheet can become a problem quickly when rates rise or sales fall.

cashFlow.title: Understand cash flow
cashFlow.text: Cash flow shows whether accounting profit turns into real money that can be reinvested, returned to shareholders, or used to protect the business.
cashFlow.details: Profit can look good on paper, but companies live on cash. Operating cash flow shows what the business generates from operations. Capital expenditures show what must be invested to maintain or grow the business. Free cash flow is what remains after important spending. It often shows the real strength of a business for shareholders.
cashFlow.example: A company may earn $1B in net income, but if it needs $900M of equipment spending every year, financial freedom is limited. Another company with the same profit and low capital needs may have much stronger free cash flow.
cashFlow.caseStudy: A SaaS company may show accounting losses while producing decent operating cash flow. A manufacturer may show profit but constantly reinvest huge amounts into factories. Each case needs context instead of one-line judgment.
cashFlow.action: Compare net income, operating cash flow, capex, and free cash flow over at least 5 years.
cashFlow.checkOne: Free cash flow is not positive only by accident for one year.
cashFlow.checkTwo: Capex is understood as maintenance or growth spending.
cashFlow.checkThree: Cash flow does not diverge from profit without explanation.
cashFlow.warning: Do not confuse a profitable company with a company that truly generates free cash.

profitability.title: Judge margins and efficiency
profitability.text: Margins, ROE, ROA, and operating efficiency show how well the business turns revenue, assets, and capital into profit.
profitability.details: Revenue without margin can be an expensive way to look large. Gross profit shows product economics, operating margin shows expense discipline, net margin shows the result after all costs, ROE shows capital efficiency, and ROA shows asset efficiency. Normal levels differ by sector, so compare margins with the company's history and peers.
profitability.example: A company can grow sales 30%, but if margin falls from 20% to 5%, shareholders should ask whether this is investment in the future or buying revenue too expensively. Growth without economics does not always create value.
profitability.caseStudy: Two companies sell similar products. One has a 25% operating margin, the other 8%. If the first keeps customers and market share, the business model may be stronger. But if the high margin falls every year, the competitive advantage may be fading.
profitability.action: Compare gross, operating, and net margins over 5 years, plus ROE/ROA against peers.
profitability.checkOne: Margins are stable or the change has a clear reason.
profitability.checkTwo: Revenue growth is not destroying profitability.
profitability.checkThree: ROE is not high only because of excessive debt.
profitability.warning: High ROE can be a trap if it comes from leverage rather than business quality.

valuation.title: Do not ignore valuation
valuation.text: Even a great business can be a poor investment at too high a price. Look at P/E, P/S, margins, growth, and alternatives.
valuation.details: Valuation asks how much you pay for quality and future growth. P/E compares price with profit, P/S compares price with revenue, P/B compares price with book value, and DCF forces you to write explicit assumptions about cash flow. No multiple works alone: a high valuation may be justified by strong growth, but dangerous if expectations are already too optimistic.
valuation.example: A company with a P/E of 45 is not automatically bad if it grows quickly, has high margins, and a long runway. But if growth slows and margins are already near a peak, that P/E may mean most good news is already priced in.
valuation.caseStudy: Two companies can both trade at P/E 25. One grows 5% with cyclical margins. The other grows 18%, has stable free cash flow, and low debt. The same multiple does not mean the same value. Valuation must be read with quality, growth, and risk.
valuation.action: Compare multiples with the company's history, competitors, and your own future growth scenario.
valuation.checkOne: P/E, P/S, or P/B are compared with growth and margins.
valuation.checkTwo: You have base, optimistic, and bad scenarios.
valuation.checkThree: You understand which expectations are already in the current price.
valuation.warning: A cheap-looking stock is not always a bargain. Sometimes a low P/E is a warning sign, not a market gift.

entry.title: Define an entry rule
entry.text: DCA reduces one-bad-timing risk, buying dips requires discipline, and lump sum does not fit everyone. Write the rule before buying.
entry.details: The entry point should not be an impulse. Different approaches exist: DCA spreads the buy over time, a limit price waits for the valuation you want, buying a dip requires predefined conditions, and lump sum can fit when the horizon is long and the money is not critical for life. The key is to know the rule before emotions start steering the action.
entry.example: Instead of buying everything today, you can decide: buy 25% now, 25% after earnings, 25% if the price reaches your valuation, and keep 25% for a better opportunity. This does not guarantee the lowest price, but it removes chaotic clicking.
entry.caseStudy: Nazar wants to buy after a 15% drop. If he does not know why it fell, this may be a trap. If the drop came from the whole market and the business is unchanged, a partial buy can make sense. If it came from lost customers, weaker guidance, or debt stress, the lower price does not make the idea better.
entry.action: Choose DCA, a limit price, or another clear entry scenario, plus the condition under which you will not buy.
entry.checkOne: The buy is staged or tied to valuation, not FOMO.
entry.checkTwo: You know what would make you add or stop.
entry.checkThree: The rule for not buying is as clear as the rule for buying.
entry.warning: Do not average down automatically. Add only when the thesis is alive, not because the red number in the portfolio feels uncomfortable.

technicalIndicators.title: Add basic technical indicators
technicalIndicators.text: Technical indicators do not replace business analysis, but they can help show trend, momentum, risk zones, and price behavior.
technicalIndicators.details: For investors, technical analysis should be a supporting layer, not the center of the decision. Moving averages can show trend, RSI can show stretched momentum, volume can show market participation, and support/resistance can mark zones where price reacted before. But an indicator does not know whether the company is expensive, whether earnings are growing, or whether the business model is broken.
technicalIndicators.example: If a quality company falls to a long-term moving average during a broad selloff, it may be worth deeper research. But if RSI is low because of a bad report and weaker guidance, oversold does not automatically mean attractive.
technicalIndicators.caseStudy: Anna sees RSI below 30 and assumes the stock must bounce. Then she learns the company lost a major customer. The indicator showed weak momentum, but the reason was fundamental. The better order is: understand the reason for the move, then use indicators for timing.
technicalIndicators.action: Choose 2-3 simple indicators for timing, but connect every buy to a fundamental thesis and valuation.
technicalIndicators.checkOne: The indicator supports entry or risk control, not the reason to own the business.
technicalIndicators.checkTwo: Volume and news context are reviewed, not only one line on a chart.
technicalIndicators.checkThree: A stop or exit scenario exists if the idea is shorter than a long-term investment.
technicalIndicators.warning: More indicators on a chart do not make the decision professional. Often they just create more noise.

risks.business: Business risk: revenue, profit, or competitive position can weaken.
risks.valuation: Valuation risk: a good company can produce poor returns if bought at a very high price.
risks.volatility: Volatility risk: stock prices can drop sharply even when the long-term story is intact.

mistakes.storyOnly: Buying only because the story sounds exciting.
mistakes.singleStock: Putting too much money into one company.
mistakes.priceVsValue: Confusing a low share price with a cheap valuation.

fit.growth: You want long-term growth and can wait through market cycles.
fit.ownership: You want to study individual businesses and their economics.
fit.longHorizon: Your goal is far enough away that short-term price swings are less dangerous.

eps.definition: Earnings per share, or profit allocated to each share.

pe.definition: Price divided by earnings. It compares what investors pay with current profits.

dividend.definition: Cash a company pays to shareholders, usually from profits.

marketCap.definition: The total market value of a company: share price multiplied by shares outstanding.

etfs-funds.title: ETFs and funds
etfs-funds.summary: Baskets of securities that let investors buy many holdings through one instrument.
etfs-funds.why: They are often the simplest way to diversify without picking every individual stock or bond.
etfs-funds.intro: An ETF or fund pools many investments into one product. It can track an index, follow a sector, hold bonds, or use an active strategy.

sections.what: Funds bundle holdings. ETFs trade during the day like stocks, while mutual funds usually price once per day.
sections.earn: Returns come from the performance of the underlying holdings, minus fees, plus any distributions.
sections.risks: Funds can still be concentrated, expensive, or poorly matched to your goal. Diversified does not mean loss-proof.
sections.mistakes: Owning many funds can still mean owning the same companies repeatedly.
sections.fit: ETFs and funds may fit simple long-term plans, monthly investing, and broad diversification.

risks.tracking: Tracking risk: the fund may not perfectly follow the index or exposure you expect.
risks.fees: Fee risk: high costs reduce returns year after year.
risks.concentration: Concentration risk: a fund name can sound broad while a few holdings dominate.

mistakes.tooMany: Buying many ETFs without checking overlap.
mistakes.feeBlind: Ignoring expense ratios and trading costs.
mistakes.overlap: Thinking two different fund names guarantee different exposure.

fit.diversification: You want broad exposure through one or a few products.
fit.simplePlan: You prefer rules and consistency over constant stock picking.
fit.monthly: You invest monthly and want an easy DCA target.

expenseRatio.definition: The annual fund cost as a percentage of assets.

indexFund.definition: A fund designed to track a market index instead of picking securities actively.

nav.definition: Net asset value, or the value of the fund holdings minus liabilities.

distribution.definition: Income or gains paid out by a fund to investors.

crypto.title: Crypto
crypto.summary: Digital assets and networks with prices driven by adoption, liquidity, sentiment, and speculation.
crypto.why: Crypto can offer exposure to new technology, but many assets have no cash flows and extreme downside risk.
crypto.intro: Crypto assets are digital tokens or network assets. Some are used to pay network fees, some claim utility, and many trade mainly on expectations.

sections.what: Crypto is not one asset class with one rulebook. Bitcoin, stablecoins, governance tokens, and NFTs have different mechanics and risks.
sections.earn: Returns usually come from price appreciation, yield programs, staking rewards, or network adoption, but none are guaranteed.
sections.risks: Prices can be extremely volatile. Custody mistakes, exchange failure, scams, smart contract bugs, and regulation can cause permanent losses.
sections.mistakes: Do not treat social media excitement as research. If you cannot explain the asset and custody risk, the position is too large.
sections.fit: Crypto may fit only a limited, high-risk part of a portfolio after emergency savings and core investments are handled.

risks.volatility: Volatility risk: large price drops can happen quickly.
risks.custody: Custody risk: lost keys, hacked wallets, or failed exchanges can mean permanent loss.
risks.cashFlow: Cash-flow risk: many tokens do not produce earnings, so valuation is harder.

mistakes.hype: Buying because a token is trending.
mistakes.keys: Ignoring wallet, exchange, and private-key security.
mistakes.allIn: Using money needed for rent, taxes, or emergency savings.

fit.smallAllocation: You can limit the position to money you can afford to lose.
fit.technologyView: You understand the technology thesis and the specific token mechanics.
fit.riskBudget: Your core plan does not depend on crypto working out.

token.definition: A digital asset issued or tracked on a blockchain network.

wallet.definition: Software or hardware used to control crypto addresses and keys.

exchange.definition: A platform where users buy, sell, or hold crypto assets.

stablecoin.definition: A token designed to track another asset, often the U.S. dollar, with its own reserve and issuer risks.

venture.title: Venture and startups
venture.summary: Private company investing where a few winners may drive most returns and many investments can fail.
venture.why: Venture can be powerful but is illiquid, hard to value, and usually unsuitable as a beginner core holding.
venture.intro: Venture investing means funding young private companies before they trade publicly. Outcomes are uncertain, timelines are long, and access is often restricted.

sections.what: Startup investments are private securities. Investors may use equity, SAFEs, convertible notes, or fund vehicles.
sections.earn: Returns usually require a future exit: acquisition, secondary sale, or IPO. Until then, quoted values can be estimates.
sections.risks: Most startups fail or dilute early investors. You may be unable to sell for years, and legal terms can heavily affect outcomes.
sections.mistakes: A great product is not enough. Cap table, terms, runway, market size, and founder execution matter.
sections.fit: Venture may fit experienced investors with high risk tolerance, long lockups, and a diversified approach.

risks.illiquidity: Illiquidity risk: you may not be able to sell when you need cash.
risks.dilution: Dilution risk: later funding rounds can reduce your ownership percentage.
risks.failure: Failure risk: many startups never become profitable or exit.

mistakes.lottery: Treating one startup as a guaranteed lottery ticket.
mistakes.noTerms: Ignoring valuation, liquidation preferences, and investor rights.
mistakes.noDiversification: Making one private investment instead of understanding portfolio math.

fit.highRisk: You can tolerate total loss on individual deals.
fit.longLockup: You do not need the money for many years.
fit.expertise: You have enough domain knowledge to evaluate the company and terms.

runway.definition: How long a company can operate before it needs more funding.

dilution.definition: A reduction in ownership percentage when new shares are issued.

valuationCap.definition: A maximum valuation used to convert some startup financing instruments into equity.

liquidity.definition: How easily an investment can be sold for cash.

cash-risk.title: Cash and risk basics
cash-risk.summary: The safety layer: emergency cash, inflation, time horizon, allocation, and rebalancing.
cash-risk.why: Good investing starts with not being forced to sell risky assets at the wrong time.
cash-risk.intro: Cash and risk basics connect every other topic. Before choosing assets, decide what money is for, when it is needed, and how much uncertainty you can handle.

sections.what: This section is about planning: emergency funds, short-term cash, inflation, risk tolerance, asset allocation, and rebalancing.
sections.earn: Cash protects flexibility. A balanced allocation earns by letting risky assets grow while safer assets support liquidity and discipline.
sections.risks: Too much cash can lose purchasing power to inflation. Too little cash can force panic selling during a downturn.
sections.mistakes: The biggest beginner mistake is investing money that should have stayed safe for near-term needs.
sections.fit: This belongs before every asset decision because it defines how much risk your plan can actually carry.

risks.inflation: Inflation risk: cash can slowly lose purchasing power.
risks.underinvesting: Underinvesting risk: staying too safe for long-term goals can make growth harder.
risks.panic: Behavior risk: no cash buffer can turn a market drop into a forced sale.

mistakes.noEmergency: Skipping an emergency fund before taking market risk.
mistakes.wrongHorizon: Using volatile assets for money needed soon.
mistakes.noRebalance: Letting the portfolio drift far from the intended risk level.

fit.foundation: You are building the base before choosing investments.
fit.shortGoals: You have near-term goals where stability matters more than return.
fit.sleep: You want a plan you can keep during bad markets.

emergencyFund.definition: Cash kept for unexpected expenses so you do not need to sell investments at a bad time.

inflation.definition: A rise in prices that reduces the purchasing power of money.

allocation.definition: How a portfolio is split across asset types such as stocks, bonds, cash, and alternatives.

rebalance.definition: Adjusting a portfolio back toward its target allocation.

register.title: Create your account
register.passwordLength: Password must be at least 8 characters.

forbidden.title: Admin access required
forbidden.description: This workspace is limited to Lucrum administrators.

calculations.title: Recent Calculations
calculations.empty: Saved calculator runs will appear here.

watchlist.title: Watchlist
watchlist.empty: Add tickers you want to follow.

admin.title: User metrics
admin.description: A compact view of Lucrum users, roles, activity, and tool usage signals.

learn.title: Learn
learn.description: Educational guides and investing basics will live here.

tools.title: Tools
tools.description: The complete calculator directory will live here.

investCalc.title: Invest Calculator
investCalc.description: A future value calculator placeholder for recurring investments.

fairPrice.title: Fair Price
fairPrice.description: A fair value and DCF calculator placeholder.

about.title: About Lucrum
about.description: Lucrum helps beginner investors understand scenarios, assumptions and basic market language before making their own decisions.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.description: Lucrum collects only the data needed to run accounts, analytics and product security. A fuller policy will document retention, cookies and user controls before paid features launch.

terms.title: Terms
terms.description: Lucrum is educational software. Calculator outputs are estimates based on user inputs and available data, not guarantees or personalized financial advice.

contact.title: Contact
contact.description: For feedback, data issues or product questions, contact the Lucrum team through the project owner while the public support channel is being prepared.

footer.disclaimer: Lucrum is for education and planning only. It does not provide investment, legal or tax advice, and calculator outputs should not be treated as a recommendation to buy or sell any asset.

notFound.title: This page does not exist.
notFound.description: The link may be old or mistyped. You can return to the tools or continue learning from the Academy.
